programs.name: Choices for Care (CFC) Waiver
programs.tagline: If your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, or medication but wants to stay home, Vermont may pay for home care services.
programs.intro:
If your parent is 65+ and needs significant daily assistance but wants to avoid nursing home care, Vermont's Choices for Care waiver can pay for home and community-based services. This Medicaid waiver program covers services that help your parent stay in their own home when they would otherwise need nursing facility care.

The program has three tiers based on need level. Only the highest need group gets guaranteed services — high and moderate need groups receive services only when state funding is available. Your parent must pass both a financial eligibility test (income up to 300% of SSI levels) and a clinical assessment proving they need nursing facility level of care.

Because this waiver requires meeting strict functional criteria, having a dementia diagnosis alone won't qualify your parent. A registered nurse must assess whether your parent needs substantial help with activities like transferring, eating, toileting, or requires skilled nursing care like wound management.

summary: Need nursing facility level of care
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must need nursing facility level of care — significant assistance with daily activities like bathing, dressing, eating, or skilled nursing care like wound management or tube feeding
otherRequirements: In-person assessment by registered nurse required

applicationGuide.summary: Apply through two separate departments — financial eligibility through DCF and functional assessment through DAIL.

steps.title: Start financial eligibility
steps.description: Contact Department for Children and Families (DCF) to begin the financial eligibility determination process.

steps.title: Schedule functional assessment
steps.description: Contact Department of Aging and Independent Living (DAIL) to arrange the required nursing assessment to determine if your parent meets nursing facility level of care.

steps.title: Gather 5 years of financial records
steps.description: Collect documentation of all financial information and any gifts or transfers of property/money from the past five years.

steps.title: Complete in-person nursing evaluation
steps.description: A registered nurse will assess your parent's functional abilities and care needs in person.

applicationGuide.waitlist: Only highest need applicants get guaranteed services. High and moderate need groups may wait for state funding availability.
applicationGuide.tip: Apply for both financial and functional eligibility simultaneously to avoid delays.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
included: Money or property given away in past 5 years

tiers.name: Highest Need
tiers.description: Guaranteed access to both nursing home and community services
tiers.coverage: Full range of home and community-based services

tiers.name: High Need
tiers.description: Services available as state resources permit
tiers.coverage: Nursing home and community services when funding available

tiers.name: Moderate Need
tiers.description: Limited services for those not yet meeting nursing home requirements
tiers.coverage: Limited services when state resources permit

categories.name: Financial Records
items: Documentation of any gifts or property transfers in past 5 years

categories.name: Medical Information
items: Recent hospital or nursing home records

contentSections.text: Only the highest need tier gets guaranteed services. High and moderate need applicants may face waiting lists depending on state funding availability.
Any money or property your parent gave away or sold below fair market value in the past 5 years can trigger a penalty period of ineligibility.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies?
faqs.answer: Yes, your parent can keep their primary home as long as they live in it (or intend to return) and the home equity doesn't exceed $730,000. The home won't count toward the asset limit.

faqs.question: What if her income is just over the limit?
faqs.answer: Your parent may be able to 'spend down' to qualify by paying medical expenses that reduce their countable income to the 300% of SSI limit.

faqs.question: Will Dad's dementia diagnosis automatically qualify him?
faqs.answer: No. A dementia diagnosis alone doesn't guarantee eligibility. A registered nurse must assess whether your father needs nursing facility level of care — meaning he needs significant help with daily activities like bathing, dressing, or eating.

faqs.question: How does this affect my parents' marriage financially?
faqs.answer: Your parent's spouse can keep income up to $2,644-$3,948 per month, and the spouse's income doesn't count toward the applicant's income limit.

faqs.question: What happens if Mom is approved but in the 'high need' category?
faqs.answer: She'll be placed on a waiting list and receive services only when state funding becomes available. Only 'highest need' applicants get immediate guaranteed access.

faqs.question: Can Dad get help immediately while we're applying?
faqs.answer: The program requires both financial approval and a clinical nursing assessment before services begin. There's no emergency or temporary coverage while applications are processing.

programs.name: Medicaid for the Aged, Blind and Disabled (ABD)
programs.tagline: If your parent is 65+ with limited income, this covers all medical care plus nursing home or home care services.
programs.intro:
If your parent is 65+ and needs help paying for medical care or long-term care, Vermont's Medicaid for the Aged, Blind and Disabled (ABD) covers everything from doctor visits to nursing home care. This program is specifically for seniors, blind individuals, or people with disabilities who meet strict income and asset limits.

Unlike regular health insurance, Medicaid ABD also covers long-term care services — both nursing home care and home-based care through Vermont's Choices for Care program. Your parent gets a care team that coordinates all services, from medical appointments to daily living assistance.

Income limits vary by location: $1,375/month for individuals living outside Chittenden County, and higher limits inside Chittenden County. Asset limits are $2,000 for a single person.

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: For long-term care services, your parent needs an assessment showing they require help with daily activities like bathing, dressing, or medication management (Activities of Daily Living).
otherRequirements: Must be aged 65+, blind, or disabled with official disability determination

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online through Vermont Health Connect by selecting Green Mountain Care.

steps.title: Apply online via Vermont Health Connect
steps.description: Go to Vermont Health Connect and apply for Green Mountain Care. Select the aged, blind, or disabled category.

steps.title: Complete form 205ALLMED
steps.description: Download and complete the specific Medicaid ABD application form from the state webpage.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Collect proof of income, assets, age, disability status, and residency before submitting.

steps.title: Schedule functional assessment (if needed)
steps.description: For long-term care services, your parent will need an assessment of their ability to perform daily living activities.

applicationGuide.tip: Chittenden County residents have higher income limits — don't assume your parent won't qualify if they live in Burlington or surrounding areas.

categories.name: Identity & Age
items: Birth certificate or other proof of age

categories.name: Medical (if applicable)
items: Functional assessment results for long-term care

contentSections.text: This is NOT the same as regular Vermont Medicaid. You must specifically apply for Medicaid ABD or through the Choices for Care program to access long-term care benefits.

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their house if they qualify?
faqs.answer: Yes, the primary home is typically exempt from the $2,000 asset limit. However, specific rules apply if your parent needs nursing home care — contact the program directly for your situation.

faqs.question: What if my parent lives in Burlington and their income is slightly higher?
faqs.answer: Chittenden County (which includes Burlington) has higher income limits than the rest of Vermont. In 2025, the limit was $1,441/month compared to $1,375 outside the county. These limits adjust annually.

faqs.question: Does this cover home care or just nursing homes?
faqs.answer: Both. Through Vermont's Choices for Care program, your parent can receive home and community-based services to help them stay in their own home, including assistance with bathing, dressing, and medication management.

faqs.question: What's the difference between this and regular Medicaid?
faqs.answer: Medicaid ABD uses stricter financial rules (SSI-related criteria) but provides access to long-term care services that regular Medicaid doesn't cover. It's specifically designed for seniors and people with disabilities.

faqs.question: My parent needs nursing home level care but wants to stay home — is that possible?
faqs.answer: Yes, if your parent qualifies for Nursing Home Level of Care (NHLOC), they can often receive equivalent services at home through the Choices for Care waiver program instead of entering a facility.

faqs.question: How do they determine if my parent is disabled enough to qualify?
faqs.answer: Vermont uses the same disability criteria as Social Security. If your parent already receives Social Security Disability, they likely qualify. Otherwise, they'll need a disability determination unless they've had continuous Medicaid eligibility since 1973.

programs.name: PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly)
programs.tagline: One program handles all your parent's medical care, day programs, and home services if they need nursing home-level care but want to stay home.

localEntities.name: Use Medicare's Find a PACE Plan tool or National PACE Association to locate Vermont providers; none explicitly named in results.[1][3]

programs.intro:
If your parent needs nursing home-level care but wants to stay in their own home, PACE provides everything under one roof: doctors, specialists, prescription drugs, day programs, home care, meals, and transportation. Because PACE coordinates all care through one team, your parent won't juggle multiple providers or worry about coverage gaps — the PACE center becomes their medical home.

Your parent must be 55+ and certified by Vermont as needing nursing home level of care (daily help with bathing, dressing, or medication management). There are no income limits to enroll, but about 90% of participants qualify for free coverage through Medicare and Medicaid. PACE is only available in specific service areas near PACE centers — it's not statewide in Vermont.

summary: Need nursing home level of care
Able to live safely at home
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must be certified by Vermont as needing nursing home level of care (a clinical assessment of whether your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, or medication management)
otherRequirements: Live in the service area of a Vermont PACE organization
Able to live safely in the community with PACE services
Not enrolled in Medicare Advantage, Medicare prescription drug plan, or hospice

applicationGuide.summary: Contact your local Vermont PACE provider directly — processing time varies by program.

steps.title: Find your local PACE provider
steps.description: Use Medicare's Find a PACE Plan tool online or contact the National PACE Association to locate Vermont providers in your area.

steps.title: Schedule an assessment
steps.description: Call the PACE center to schedule a nursing home level of care assessment with Vermont's certification team.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Bring proof of age, residency, medical records, and Medicare/Medicaid cards if you have them.

steps.title: Complete enrollment
steps.description: Once certified for nursing home level of care, enroll directly with the PACE provider.

applicationGuide.waitlist: Waitlists are common due to capped federal and state funding — varies by program.
applicationGuide.tip: Start the process early, as waitlists are common and you must live near a PACE center.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits for Free Coverage
excluded: Burial funds up to certain limits

categories.name: Identity & Residency
items: Proof of Vermont residency (utility bill, lease)

categories.name: Medical
items: Medical records for nursing home level of care assessment

categories.name: Financial (for free coverage)
items: Asset documentation if seeking Medicaid eligibility

contentSections.text: PACE is only available in specific service areas near PACE centers. Check if your parent lives in a covered area before starting the application process.
Private pay option exists (typically $7,000+/month) if your parent doesn't qualify for Medicaid but still needs comprehensive care coordination.

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their house if they qualify for PACE?
faqs.answer: Yes. Your parent's primary home doesn't count toward asset limits for Medicaid eligibility, and PACE is specifically designed to help people stay in their own homes instead of moving to a nursing facility.

faqs.question: What if my parent already has Medicare Advantage?
faqs.answer: They'll need to disenroll from Medicare Advantage before joining PACE. PACE becomes their sole source of Medicare and Medicaid benefits, so they can't have overlapping coverage.

faqs.question: How far does my parent need to live from a PACE center?
faqs.answer: Your parent must live in the service area of a PACE organization and be able to get to the PACE center regularly for medical care and day programs. PACE provides transportation, but distance limits vary by provider.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is too high for free coverage?
faqs.answer: There are no income limits to enroll in PACE itself. If your parent doesn't qualify for Medicaid, they can still participate by paying privately (typically $7,000+/month) or using Medicare for covered services.

faqs.question: Can my parent choose their own doctor with PACE?
faqs.answer: No. PACE assigns a care team that includes doctors, nurses, and specialists. All medical care must go through the PACE program — you can't see outside providers except in emergencies.

faqs.question: What happens if there's a waitlist?
faqs.answer: Waitlists are common due to funding caps. Your parent can stay on the waitlist while continuing their current care. Contact the PACE center regularly for updates on availability.
